examples.title: DSO: Days Sales Outstanding
examples.definition: DSO measures the average number of days a company takes to collect payment after a sale. It is the primary KPI for Accounts Receivable efficiency.
examples.why_it_matters: CFOs use DSO to diagnose cash conversion lag and AR process health. A rising DSO signals collections risk or loose credit policy. It is the first metric I build in any AR dashboard.

key_concepts.explanation: The actual days between invoice date and payment receipt
More accurate for seasonal businesses — works back from period-end AR through most recent invoices to match the AR balance

formulas.name: Simple DSO
formulas.expression: DSO = (Accounts Receivable / Total Credit Sales) × Number of Days
formulas.notes: Use with caution for seasonal businesses

formulas.name: Countback DSO (Best Practice)
formulas.expression: Work backward from AR balance through monthly sales until balance is exhausted — count the days consumed
formulas.notes: More accurate for variable revenue patterns

best_practices: Always segment DSO by customer tier, region, and payment terms before aggregating
Use countback method for retail or seasonal clients
common_mistakes: Using total sales (including cash) instead of credit sales in denominator
Mixing invoiced date vs shipped date inconsistently
practical_applications: AR Dashboard in Power BI: top KPI card with trend sparkline and monthly target line
CFO reporting pack: week-over-week DSO movement with drill-through by customer
